m_Item1.QuestionText: Bean defined in spring framework by default
m_Item1.Explanation: Default scope of single bean definition to single object instance per spring IoC container

m_Item1.QuestionText: When a bean is scoped as a ____________, a new instance is created each time the bean is requested from the Spring container.
m_Item1.Explanation: When a bean is scoped as a prototype, a new instance is created each time the bean is requested from the Spring container. This is ideal for beans that don't maintain state or perform expensive initialization logic.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What are the different of  auto wiring types?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Autowiring types are :
                                  byName,byType, constructor and no

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true ?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Interface ApplicationContext
package : org.springframework.contextusper 
Super interfaces:  ApplicationEventPublisher, BeanFactory, EnvironmentCapable, HierarchicalBeanFactory, ListableBeanFactory, MessageSource, ResourceLoader, ResourcePatternResolver
 All Known Subinterfaces: ConfigurableApplicationContext, ConfigurableWebApplicationContext, WebApplicationContext

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is no mode of autowiring?

Options.OptionText: Default setting which means no autowiring and you should use explicit bean reference for wiring.
Spring first tries to wire using autowire by constructor, if it does not work, Spring tries to autowire by byType.
Similar to byType, but type applies to constructor arguments.

m_Item1.Explanation: no mode of autowiring is the default mode which means no autowiring and you should use explicit bean reference for wiring.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which out of the following is true about spring core container?

Options.OptionText: The main task of spring core container is DI using setter methods or construtors.
The spring core container uses the so-called bean factory to create new objects
The Spring core container handles the configuration, generally based on annotations or on an XML file

m_Item1.Explanation: All options are correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the following Spring configuration file, what is the correct answer:
<bean class="com.spring.service.MyServiceImpl">
   <property name="repository" ref="jpaDao"/>
</bean>
<bean class="com.spring.repository.JpaDao"/>

Options.OptionText: The first declared bean MyServiceImpl is missing an id must be named myService
The second declared bean JpaDao is missing an id must be named jpaDao
A and B both are rights .
A and B both are wrong.

m_Item1.Explanation: The second declared bean JpaDao is missing an id must be named jpaDao .

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What the name of the bean defined in the following configuration class?
     Select a single answer.
    @Configuration
    public class ApplicationConfig {
        @Autowired
        private DataSource dataSource;
        @Bean
        ClientRepository clientRepository() {
            ClientRepository accountRepository = new JpaClientRepository();
            accountRepository.setDataSource(dataSource);
            return accountRepository;
        }
    }

Options.OptionText: Two beans are defined: a data souce and a repository

m_Item1.Explanation: clientRepository is name of the bean defined in configuration class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What statement is not correct in live environment? Select a unique answer.

Options.OptionText: Constuctor and properties autowiring in the same bean are not compatible
A bean should have a default or a no-args constructor
The <constructor-arg> tag could take type, name and index to reduce ambiguity

m_Item1.Explanation: All of the options are correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is/are typically case(s) where you usually need to manually instantiated an ApplicationContext?

Options.OptionText: In an integration test running with the SpringJUnit4ClassRunner
In a standalone application started with a main method

m_Item1.Explanation: In a standalone application started with a main method then usually we need to manually instantiate an ApplicationContext.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Select the right statement about referring a Spring configuration file inside the package
com.example.myapp in the below example?
ApplicationContext context = new
ClassPathXmlApplicationContext("classpath:/com.example.myapp.config.xml");

Options.OptionText: Package name using the dot character is compulsory.
The slash character preceding com.example is recommended

m_Item1.Explanation: starting with a slash (/) is the more robust and common way to ensure the resource is found relative to the root of any classpath entry

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not a type of autowiring in Spring?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Spring, autowiring is a feature that allows Spring to automatically inject dependencies into beans. The three types of autowiring supported by Spring are byName, byType, and constructor. These types determine how Spring resolves and injects dependencies. However, there is no autowiring type called "byProperty" in Spring

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which tag is used in XML configuration for managing the lifecycle of a bean by containers in Spring?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Spring XML configuration, the <bean> tag is used to define a bean, including its lifecycle and other properties. Within the <bean> tag, you can specify attributes such as scope to manage the lifecycle of the bean. Additionally, you can define initialization and destruction methods using the init-method and destroy-method attributes respectively to further control the lifecycle of the bean.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which annotation is commonly used for dependencies management in Spring framework?
m_Item1.Explanation: @Autowired annotation is used for wiring dependencies of the beans

m_Item1.QuestionText:
In a Spring bean definition XML file, which line represents dependency injection?
/* line1 */<bean id="edao" class="com.dao.EmployeeDAO" />
/* line2 */<bean id="eService" class="com.example.EmployeeService">
/* line3 */   <property name="empdao" ref="edao"/>
/* line4 */   <property name="sname" value="DBService" />
/* line5 */</bean>
m_Item1.Explanation: When property of one bean refers to some another bean using ref attribute, it is called dependency injection

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a Spring bean definition XML file, which line represents dependency injection?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Spring XML configuration, dependency injection is commonly performed using the <property> tag within the <bean> definition. The name attribute specifies the name of the property in the bean class, while the ref attribute specifies the bean to inject into that property. This line represents dependency injection by injecting the dependencyBean into the dependency property of the exampleBean. Option C represents constructor-based dependency injection, not property-based. Option D is not a valid Spring XML tag.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If the getBean() method in Spring is called 3 times, how many times does the bean get created?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Spring, beans have singleton scope by default. So they are created only once per Spring container context. When getBean() is called for a singleton bean, Spring checks if the bean has already been created. If it has, it returns the existing instance; otherwise, it creates a new instance. Since singleton beans are cached after creation, subsequent calls to getBean() for the same bean definition return the cached instance. Therefore, regardless of how many times getBean() is called, the singleton bean is instantiated only once.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if there are multiple beans of the same type when using @Autowired?

Options.OptionText: Spring automatically injects the bean with the longest name.
You'll encounter a runtime error due to ambiguity.
The first bean definition encountered in the configuration is used.
You can use @BeanName to specify the desired bean implementation.

m_Item1.Explanation: If there are multiple beans of the same type and no further specification, Spring will throw an exception due to ambiguity. .

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary function of the @Autowired annotation in Spring?

Options.OptionText: It automatically creates beans for all classes in your application.
It marks a field, constructor argument, or setter method for automatic dependency injection by Spring.
It enforces security by restricting access to certain dependencies.
It defines the scope (singleton, prototype) of a bean.

m_Item1.Explanation: @Autowired is used to mark fields, constructor arguments, or setter methods for automatic dependency injection by Spring.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which are valid method signatures for the method ClassPathXmlApplicationContext.getBean():
Select all that apply:
A: Object getBean(String name) throws BeansException.
B: <T> T getBean(String name, Class<T> requiredType) throws BeansException.
C: <T> T getBean(String name, String requiredType) throws BeansException.
D: <T> T getBean(Class<T> requiredType) throws BeansException.
m_Item1.Explanation: We can access the beans by using bean name(bean is in the configuration) or bean class

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is byType mode of autowiring?

Options.OptionText: Default setting which means no autowiring and you should use explicit bean reference for wiring.
Autowiring by property name. Spring tries to match and wire its properties with the beans defined by the same names in the configuration file
Spring first tries to wire using autowire by constructor, if it does not work, Spring tries to autowire by byType.
Autowiring by property type. Spring tries to match and wire a property if its type matches with exactly one of the beans name in configuration file.

m_Item1.Explanation: Bean definition type will be matched with the type of property at the time of bean creation but there has to be only one bean of the respective type.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following has the default scope in Spring?
m_Item1.Explanation: Default scope in Spring is Singleton.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following modules is not used in Spring?
m_Item1.Explanation: Application Module is not used in Spring.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following methods allows to create objects for a class?
m_Item1.Explanation: All are valid ways for creating objects of class

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an IOC or Dependency injection?
m_Item1.Explanation: IOC or Dependency Injection is a design pattern

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true for Controllers in Spring?
m_Item1.Explanation: Task of the controller is to map request to controller method, handle request, generate model and view.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following classes can you use for executing the  SQL queries in Spring?
m_Item1.Explanation: JdbcTemplate classes can used for executing the SQL queries in spring.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not a component of the Spring framework?
m_Item1.Explanation: All are supported by spring framework.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is Dependency Injection?

Options.OptionText: A design pattern which implements Inversion of Control for software applications.
A technique to get dependencies of any project.
Used to promote tight coupling in code.

m_Item1.Explanation: A design pattern which implements Inversion of Control for software applications.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following spring module provide the capability of DI or IOC
m_Item1.Explanation: Spring core container is responsible for implementation of DI

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following Java Collection types can be injected in a Spring application?

Options.OptionText: Using list, collection, map or props tag
Using list, set, map or props tag
Using list, set, map or collection tag
Using list, set, props or collection tag

m_Item1.Explanation: Java Collection types :  list,set,map or props tag can be injected. Collection tag is not available

m_Item1.QuestionText:
When a lazy-initialized bean is a dependency of a singleton bean that is not lazy-initialized, the
ApplicationContext creates the lazy-initialized bean at startup
m_Item1.Explanation: By default, Spring eagerly creates and configures all singleton beans during application context initialization.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which class is used for loading Spring bean definitions from XML configuration files located on the classpath and creating the corresponding Spring beans within the application context.
m_Item1.Explanation: ClassPathApplicationContext  class  is used to read bean configurations writen in a file present on the classpath.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What are the types of Dependency Injection Spring supports?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Spring Supports following ways of Dependency Injection :
                                                     Setter injection
                                                     Constructor injection

m_Item1.QuestionText: Annotation to force dependency injection is____
m_Item1.Explanation: Annotation to force dependency injection is  @Autowired .

m_Item1.QuestionText: The process of applying aspects to a target object to create a new proxy object in spring ascpets is called as_____
m_Item1.Explanation: The process of applying aspects to a target object to create a new proxy object is called as  Weaving .

m_Item1.QuestionText:
In order to use @Component annotation for a bean u need to add following tag in Spring bean
configuration file.
m_Item1.Explanation: <context:component-scan>  tag used in spring bean configuration file.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the Key strategy of spring framework?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Key stratergy of spring framework :
                                1. Application development in POJO style.
                                2. Loose coupling through Dl.
                                3. Declarative programming using AOP

m_Item1.QuestionText: Spring controller can be defined using annotation
m_Item1.Explanation: To define controller in Spring Application used @Controller annotation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following  is a root interface which represents basic IoC container in spring?
m_Item1.Explanation:
The root interface for accessing a Spring bean container.
The BeanFactory is the actual container which instantiates, configures, and manages a number of beans.
The most used API that implements the BeanFactory is the XmlBeanFactory
ApplicationContext extends the BeanFactory interface. Hence, the ApplicationContext includes all functionality of the BeanFactory and much more

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is spring?

Options.OptionText: Spring is an open source development framework for enterprise Java.
Spring is a development framework for .Net applications.
Spring is a development framework for PHP based applications.

m_Item1.Explanation: Spring is an open source development framework for enterprise Java.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statements are true about DispatcherServlet ?

Options.OptionText: DispatcherServlet is an expression of the 'Front Controller' design pattern
The DispatcherServlet is the actual Servlet .
DispatcherServlet is declared in the web.xml of your web application.
The Spring DispatcherServlet has a couple of special beans it uses in order to be able to process requests and render the appropriate views. These beans can be configured in WebApplicationContext.

m_Item1.Explanation: DispatcherServlet is front controller where all requests are landing first and then processed by respective controllers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true for JNDI ?
m_Item1.Explanation: E

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which are the modules of core container?
m_Item1.Explanation: The container will create the objects, wire them together, configure them, and manage their complete life cycle from creation till destruction.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
package com.beans;
public class TestBean {
  private int year;
  private String happy;
  public TestBean( String happy,int year) {
    this.year = year;
    this.happy = happy;
  }
}
What is the correct way to write constructor injection?

Options.OptionText:
<bean name='testClass' class='com.beans.TestBean'>
<constructor-arg type='java.lang.String' value='Happy new year'/>
<constructor-arg type='java.lang.String' value='2015'/>
</bean>

<bean name='testClass' class='com.beans.TestBean'>
<constructor-arg index='0' value='Happy new year'/>
<constructor-arg index='1' value='2015'/>
</bean>

<bean name='testClass' class='com.beans.TestBean'>
<constructor-arg index='1' value='Happy new year'/>
<constructor-arg index='0' value='2015'/>
</bean>

<bean name='testClass' class='com.beans.TestBean'>
<constructor-arg type='java.lang.Integer' value='2015'/>
<constructor-arg type='java.lang.String' value='Happy new year'/>
</bean>

m_Item1.Explanation: for int type of property type should be mentioned as "int". If index is used without type its sequence can be altered

m_Item1.QuestionText:
@Service
public class MyService {
    @Autowired
    private MyRepository repository;

    // ...
}
What will happen if the MyRepository bean is not found in the Spring context?

Options.OptionText: The MyService bean will be created ignoring repository
The MyService bean will be created, but the repository field will be null
The application will continue to run without any issues

m_Item1.Explanation: If the MyRepository bean is not found, Spring will throw a NoSuchBeanDefinitionException because it cannot fulfill the dependency injection requirement.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main benefit of using Dependency Injection (DI) in a Spring application?

Options.OptionText: It promotes loose coupling between the objects.
It enforces a rigid architecture pattern.

m_Item1.Explanation: Dependency Injection allows objects to be loosely coupled by injecting dependencies at runtime rather than hard coding them, making the application more modular and easier to maintain.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which interface in Spring is used to indicate that a bean is capable of configuring itself after all its properties have been set?
m_Item1.Explanation: The InitializingBean interface in Spring provides a way for beans to perform some initialization after all properties have been set, through the afterPropertiesSet() method.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Spring, what is the primary role of the ApplicationContext container?

Options.OptionText: To provide a file system for the application.
To manage the lifecycle of the beans.

m_Item1.Explanation: The ApplicationContext container in Spring is responsible for instantiating, configuring, and assembling the beans, managing their lifecycle.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you inject a value directly into a Spring bean using annotations?
m_Item1.Explanation: @Value is used to inject a value directly into a Spring bean field. This can be used for injecting simple values like strings, literals, etc.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which generic stereotype annotation is used to automatically detect and register classes as Spring beans?
m_Item1.Explanation: The @Component annotation in Spring is used to indicate that a class should be automatically detected and registered as a bean within the Spring application context.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following types of dependency injection is described as having the container call directly into the object's setter methods to provide dependencies?
m_Item1.Explanation: Setter-based injection uses setter methods to inject dependencies into a Spring bean after it is instantiated, allowing for optional dependencies and reconfiguration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the @Qualifier annotation in Spring?

Options.OptionText: To ensure a bean is a singleton.
To specify the injection type for a dependency.
To resolve ambiguity in bean wiring when multiple beans of the same type exist.
To define the lifecycle of a bean.

m_Item1.Explanation: The @Qualifier annotation is used in Spring to resolve the ambiguity when multiple beans of the same type are present, by specifying the exact bean name to be injected.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the bean scope that returns a new instance of the bean every time it is requested?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'prototype' scope in Spring indicates that a new bean instance will be created each time it is requested. This is in contrast to the 'singleton' scope which returns the same instance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a valid way to define and manage beans in Spring?
m_Item1.Explanation: Spring beans can be defined using XML-based configuration, annotations, or Java configuration classes. REST API is not a method used for defining beans in Spring.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a primary disadvantage of using field-based dependency injection?

Options.OptionText: It does not allow final fields.
It cannot be used with Spring Boot.

m_Item1.Explanation: Field-based dependency injection does not allow the use of final fields because it relies on reflection to set values, and final fields must be set in the constructor.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Spring, which scope is ideal for defining a bean that needs to cope with concurrent requests?
m_Item1.Explanation: A 'singleton' bean scope is thread-safe only if the bean itself is designed to handle concurrent access. Otherwise, choosing a 'request' or 'prototype' scope can ensure no shared instances across threads.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is true about dependency injection in Spring?

Options.OptionText: Dependency injection can only be performed through constructors in Spring.
Setter-based injection is mandatory in Spring.
Spring allows for both constructor and setter-based dependency injection.

m_Item1.Explanation: Spring supports multiple types of dependency injection, including constructor-based and setter-based, allowing flexibility in bean configuration according to the application's needs.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following annotations is used to remove a bean from Spring's scope after processing?
m_Item1.Explanation: The @PreDestroy annotation is used in Spring to specify a method to be called just before a bean is destroyed, allowing cleanup activities to take place.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a typical advantage of Dependency Injection?
m_Item1.Explanation: While dependency injection typically provides benefits such as improved modularity, easier testing, and reduced boilerplate code, it does not inherently increase code complexity. In some cases, it simplifies complexity by decoupling components.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main purpose of the @Autowired annotation?

Options.OptionText: To declare a method as a factory method for beans.
To automatically wire beans based on type.
To specify a custom bean lifespan.
To configure a bean for remote access.

m_Item1.Explanation: The @Autowired annotation in Spring is used to automatically inject dependencies by type, and it can be applied to constructors, methods, and fields within a bean.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Spring, what is the use of the @ComponentScan annotation?

Options.OptionText: To specify the package to scan for components.
To designate a class as a Spring-managed bean.
To configure a bean with a specific lifecycle.

m_Item1.Explanation: The @ComponentScan annotation in Spring specifies the base packages to scan for components (classes annotated with @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller, etc.), enabling their automatic registration as beans.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is typically used in Spring to manage transaction boundaries declaratively?
m_Item1.Explanation: The @Transactional annotation in Spring is used to manage transaction demarcation declaratively. It can be applied to classes or methods to enable transaction management.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does Spring handle a bean lifecycle in practice?

Options.OptionText: By controlling only the creation and destruction phases.
By providing hooks for initializing and destruction callbacks.
By applying aspect-oriented programming techniques exclusively.

m_Item1.Explanation: Spring handles the life cycle of a bean through the initialization, post-configuration, and destruction phase, allowing developers to provide hooks for actions to be taken at each phase.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which bean scope would be suitable for a shopping cart implemented as a Spring bean?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'session' scope in Spring means that the bean is tied to the lifespan of an HTTP session. This makes it suitable for stateful beans like a shopping cart, which are specific to the user session.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When using component scanning in Spring, which of the following annotations is not typically automatically detected?
m_Item1.Explanation: The annotations @Service, @Controller, and @Repository are commonly detected during component scanning. There is no standard @DisabledBean annotation in Spring for component scanning.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is false regarding Spring's ApplicationContext?

Options.OptionText: It is responsible for initializing the beans.
It manages the lifecycle of beans.
It replaces the need for a database connection.
It can publish events to beans that are registered as listeners.

m_Item1.Explanation: The Spring ApplicationContext does not replace the need for a database connection; rather, it initializes and manages the lifecycle of the beans and acts as the central interface to the Spring IoC container.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a common feature provided by IOC containers?
m_Item1.Explanation: Dynamic polymorphism is a feature related to object-oriented programming languages and is not specifically provided by IOC containers. IOC containers focus on managing dependencies and application structure.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the difference between 'bean factory' and 'application context' in the Spring IOC container?

Options.OptionText: Bean factory is a more advanced version of application context
Bean factory doesn’t support internationalization but application context does
Application context is not part of the Spring framework
Bean factory supports autowiring; application context does not

m_Item1.Explanation: Bean factory is the basic container with simpler functionalities, whereas application context adds more enterprise-specific functionalities like internationalization and event propagation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which phase comes first when using an IOC container, configuration or instantiation of beans?
m_Item1.Explanation: Configuration of beans is the first phase since the container needs to know how and what to instantiate in terms of dependencies and other configurations.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of IOC, what does 'dependency lookup' refer to?

Options.OptionText: Manually retrieving dependencies from the container

m_Item1.Explanation: Dependency lookup refers to the application code manually retrieving the required dependencies from the IOC container as opposed to dependency injection which automatically provides them.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which type of Dependency Injection is often considered best for mandatory dependencies?
m_Item1.Explanation: Constructor Injection is often considered best for mandatory dependencies because it ensures that dependencies are provided at the time of object creation, making them immediately available and final.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What advantage does Setter Injection have over Constructor Injection?

Options.OptionText: It requires all dependencies at object creation

m_Item1.Explanation: Setter Injection allows for optional dependencies since setters can be called conditionally after object creation, unlike Constructor Injection which requires all dependencies to be provided at construction time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does Interface Injection differ from Constructor and Setter Injection?

Options.OptionText: It injects dependencies via method parameters
It uses factory methods for dependencies
It requires implementing interface methods for injection
It does not require any DI container

m_Item1.Explanation: Interface Injection requires the client to implement an interface that exposes a method for the injection, whereas Constructor and Setter Injection use constructors and setters for dependency provision.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is true about Constructor Injection?

Options.OptionText: It is generally more compatible with frameworks than Setter Injection
It does not support cyclic dependencies
It is used for resolving dependencies at runtime
It allows dependencies to be injected after object creation

m_Item1.Explanation: Constructor Injection does not support cyclic dependencies since each object in the cycle would need to be created before the other, which is not possible. It provides dependencies at creation time and not at runtime.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a downside of using Interface Injection?

Options.OptionText: It increases the complexity of unit testing
It requires changes in existing code to implement interfaces
It restricts the use of final classes

m_Item1.Explanation: Interface Injection requires implementing specific interface methods for dependency provisioning, meaning changes must be made to existing code, which can be a downside if existing classes need to be modified.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a key benefit of using Dependency Injection?

Options.OptionText: It enhances code reusability and flexibility
It reduces performance by increasing the number of objects
It eliminates the use of design patterns completely

m_Item1.Explanation: Dependency Injection enhances code reusability and flexibility by decoupling the creation of objects from their usage, which also makes the system more maintainable and adaptable to changes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the default scope of a bean in Spring Framework?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Spring, the default scope of a bean is 'singleton', meaning that a single instance of the bean will be created and shared across the entire application context.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which bean scope should be used in a web application to ensure a new bean instance is created for each HTTP request?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'request' scope in Spring creates a new bean instance for each HTTP request, making it ideal for web applications where each request should be treated independently.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scope is specific to a web application running in Spring and creates a bean instance for each HTTP session?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'session' scope in Spring is specific to web applications and ensures that a new bean instance is created for each HTTP session.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What scope would you use if you need a bean to be instantiated for each HTTP request and discarded afterward in a web application?
m_Item1.Explanation: Using the 'request' scope will create a new bean instance for each HTTP request, and the bean will be discarded once the request is complete. This is useful for request-scoped data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of autowiring in Spring's dependency injection?

Options.OptionText: To automatically inject objects by type

m_Item1.Explanation: Autowiring in Spring's dependency injection is primarily used for automatically injecting beans into the dependent classes by type, which helps to minimize the need for explicit configuration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which autowiring mode injects bean properties by matching them with beans in the application context according to property names?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'byName' autowiring mode injects bean properties by matching them with beans in the application context that have the same names.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a bean has multiple constructor arguments, which autowiring mode should be used to choose the constructor with maximum arguments?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'constructor' autowiring mode in Spring tries to match its arguments, by type, with the beans in the application context, thereby selecting the constructor with the maximum number of arguments.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which annotation is commonly used to enable autowiring in Spring?
m_Item1.Explanation: The '@Autowired' annotation in Spring is commonly used to enable autowiring of dependencies into a bean.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if Spring cannot find a suitable bean for autowiring?

Options.OptionText: An error is thrown during application start-up.
Spring creates a new bean automatically.
The property is set to null.
The application continues with default configurations.

m_Item1.Explanation: If Spring cannot find a suitable bean for autowiring, it throws a BeanCreationException, which prevents the application from starting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which autowiring mode is deprecated in Spring and no longer recommended?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'autodetect' mode has been deprecated in Spring since version 2.5 and is no longer recommended for use. It attempted to use 'constructor' or 'byType' modes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you disambiguate beans that have the same type but different names when using autowiring?

Options.OptionText: By autowiring only the first occurrence

m_Item1.Explanation: The '@Qualifier' annotation is used in Spring to disambiguate beans when there are multiple beans of the same type, allowing you to specify which bean to inject.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the role of the ApplicationContext in Spring?

Options.OptionText: It is used to compile Java code.
It stores and manages application metadata.
It is responsible for scanning configuration files.
It is responsible for managing beans and their lifecycle.

m_Item1.Explanation: ApplicationContext is responsible for managing beans and their lifecycle, providing advanced features such as event propagation, declarative mechanisms, etc.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does Spring IOC container manage the lifecycle of beans?
m_Item1.Explanation: Spring IOC container uses BeanFactory and ApplicationContext to manage the creation, lifecycle, and destruction of beans automatically.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What role does the @Autowired annotation play in Spring?
m_Item1.Explanation: The @Autowired annotation in Spring specifies automatic dependency injection, allowing the Spring framework to automatically resolve and inject the required bean.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you specify the preferred candidate for dependency injection when multiple beans are eligible?
m_Item1.Explanation: The @Qualifier annotation is used in Spring to specify which bean should be given preference when multiple beans of the same type are available for dependency injection.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main difference between BeanFactory and ApplicationContext in Spring?

Options.OptionText: BeanFactory loads beans at startup, whereas ApplicationContext loads them lazily.
ApplicationContext supports internationalization, whereas BeanFactory does not.
BeanFactory supports annotation-based configuration, whereas ApplicationContext does not.
There is no difference between them.

m_Item1.Explanation: ApplicationContext supports additional features like internationalization, event propagation, and allows for declarative placeholder replacement, which BeanFactory does not support.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the default scope of a Spring Bean?
m_Item1.Explanation: Singleton is the default scope for a Spring Bean, meaning only one instance is created per Spring IoC container.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the @Autowired annotation do in Spring?

Options.OptionText: It creates a new bean instance
It injects a bean from the application context
It defines a new Spring aspect
It persists data to the database

m_Item1.Explanation: The @Autowired annotation in Spring is used for automatic injection of beans from the application context into a class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which annotation is used to declare a class as a Spring configuration class?
m_Item1.Explanation: The @Configuration annotation is used to indicate that a class can be used by the Spring IoC container as a source of bean definitions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Spring, which of the following is a way to inject dependencies into a bean using constructor injection?

Options.OptionText: Using the @Autowired annotation on a field
Providing a constructor with parameters in the bean class
Using a setter method with the @Inject annotation
Using the @Resource annotation on a method

m_Item1.Explanation: Constructor injection in Spring is accomplished by defining a constructor in the bean class with parameters representing the dependencies to be injected.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which Spring annotation is used to indicate that a method should have its parameters injected with dependencies?
m_Item1.Explanation: @Autowired annotation is used in Spring to indicate that a method, constructor, or field declaration is to be autowired by Spring’s dependency injection facilities.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Spring, what scope would you choose for a bean to ensure a new instance is created each time it is requested?
m_Item1.Explanation: The prototype scope in Spring creates a new instance of the bean each time it is requested from the container.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you explicitly declare a bean in Spring without using annotations?

Options.OptionText: By using a configuration properties file
By writing a manual instantiation in main method

m_Item1.Explanation: Beans can be declared in Spring using XML configuration by defining bean elements inside the XML file.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following provides a way to manually specify the order of bean initialization and destruction in Spring?

Options.OptionText: The init-method and destroy-method attributes in XML configuration

m_Item1.Explanation: In the Spring XML configuration, you can use the init-method and destroy-method attributes to specify the methods for initialization and destruction.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which annotation is used for field injection in Spring?
m_Item1.Explanation: @Autowired is used for field injection to inject dependencies directly into the fields of a class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the role of the @Qualifier annotation in Spring dependency injection?

Options.OptionText: It is used to define a bean in a configuration class.
It specifies an alias for a bean in XML configuration.
It is used to distinguish between different beans of the same type.
It is used to denote a bean for lazy initialization.

m_Item1.Explanation: @Qualifier annotation in Spring helps to narrow down the candidate beans when there are multiple beans of the same type available, ensuring the correct one is injected.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following are valid autowiring modes in XML configuration?
m_Item1.Explanation: In XML configuration, autowiring modes include 'byType', 'byName', 'constructor', and 'autodetect'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which interface should be implemented if you want to control the autowiring externally in Spring?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'InstantiationAwareBeanPostProcessor' interface allows for custom autowire logic by intervening in the bean instantiation process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if no autowire candidate is found in Spring for @Autowired?
m_Item1.Explanation: If no autowire candidate is found, Spring throws a runtime exception indicating an unsatisfied dependency.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a bean is defined with 'autowire-candidate=false', what does it mean?

Options.OptionText: The bean can still be autowired.
The bean is ignored for autowiring.
The bean will be used twice.
The bean must be defined in other beans.

m_Item1.Explanation: Setting 'autowire-candidate=false' on a bean prevents it from being considered as a candidate for autowiring.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does Spring resolve ambiguity between two beans marked with @Primary during autowiring?

Options.OptionText: Spring cannot resolve it and ignores both

m_Item1.Explanation: When ambiguity exists despite @Primary, Spring can use @Qualifier on one of the beans to resolve which one to wire.
